skill.name: python-env
skill.description: Guides Python developers through uv-based virtual environments, dependency management, pyproject.toml setup, and package publishing workflows.
skill.summary: Manage Python environments, dependencies, and package publishing with uv.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, network, filesystem, and environment indicators. Most command hits are false positives from Markdown code fences and legitimate uv examples, but the remote shell installer and credential-handling examples are real marketplace risks. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked, but it should not publish until risky guidance is revised.

high_findings.title: Remote Shell Installer Instruction
high_findings.description: The compatibility line recommends downloading an installer over HTTPS and piping it directly into a shell. This pattern executes network-delivered code immediately and can lead to arbitrary code execution if the endpoint, transport, or copied command is compromised.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present in the skill metadata. It is installation guidance rather than hidden malicious code, but the execution risk is direct and well understood.

high_findings.title: Credential Exposure in Package Configuration Examples
high_findings.description: The references show package index credentials in URLs and PyPI tokens in a home-directory configuration file. The values are placeholders, but the examples encourage command and file patterns that can leak tokens through shell history, logs, or filesystem access.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples contain placeholder tokens, so there is no evidence of real secret exfiltration. Confidence is high enough because the documented pattern is unsafe if copied into real workflows.

medium_findings.title: Broad Bash-Based Environment Changes
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash support and teaches commands that create virtual environments, install packages, run scripts, sync dependencies, build distributions, and upload packages. These actions are legitimate for this skill but can modify project state, execute local code, and contact package services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly documented and aligned with Python environment management. The risk is operational rather than malicious, because the skill relies on user-visible shell commands.

medium_findings.title: Non-Standard Package Index Guidance
medium_findings.description: The dependency and publishing references include private package indexes and TestPyPI installation commands. These sources can be valid, but copied blindly they can install untrusted packages or create dependency-confusion exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The package index examples are intentional documentation, not stealth network calls. The risk remains meaningful because package index choice changes the trust boundary for dependency installation.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are Markdown code fences or inline command examples, not Ruby backtick execution. They are user-visible documentation examples and do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown tables or fenced examples. No executable source file or hidden runtime hook is present in the scanned file list.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Several URL findings are example project links, package index placeholders, or official test package infrastructure. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context identifies these as project metadata or changelog examples. They are static documentation, not network execution code.

low_findings.title: Environment File Library Mention Only
low_findings.description: The dotenv-related static hit is a dependency name in a FastAPI pyproject example. No evidence found that the skill reads, prints, or transmits environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only lists python-dotenv as a dependency. There is no code path in the scanned files that accesses environment values.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations are ordinary Markdown or packaging text, such as descriptions, README references, and review guidance. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or any cryptographic operation in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic code or hash function usage. This appears to be substring matching against documentation text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Installer to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a curl-to-shell installation command. Marketplace guidance should prefer verified package manager instructions or explicit download, inspect, then execute steps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present verbatim in metadata and is a known high-risk installation style.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credentials Embedded in Package Index URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The dependency guide shows username and token values embedded in index URLs and an environment variable. This can expose secrets through process lists, logs, shell history, and copied configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credentials are placeholders, but the documented shape is a real secret-handling anti-pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Home Directory Token Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The publishing guide documents a PyPI token file under the user home directory. This can be valid for twine, but it requires strict permissions and safer alternatives are already mentioned later.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The guidance is explicit and security-relevant, but it is documentation rather than hidden access to the user home directory.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Upload Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The publishing guide includes commands that upload distribution artifacts to package repositories. These commands can publish unintended artifacts if run in the wrong project or account context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are legitimate publishing steps. The risk depends on user context and credentials, so confidence is moderate.

content.value_statement: Python projects often need repeatable environments, dependency locks, and packaging steps. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical uv workflows for setup, dependency management, and publishing.
actual_capabilities: Creates uv virtual environment workflows for new and existing Python projects.
Shows package install, uninstall, sync, and lock-file commands using uv.
Provides pyproject.toml patterns for libraries, CLIs, FastAPI apps, and tooling.
Explains workspace, local dependency, Git dependency, and private index patterns.
Outlines build, TestPyPI, PyPI, versioning, changelog, and CI publishing steps.
limitations: Requires the uv CLI to be installed before most workflows can run.
Provides guidance and command examples, not automated validation of package trust.
Some commands can modify environments, install packages, or upload artifacts.
Private package and token examples need safer secret handling before production use.

use_cases.title: Create a Python Project Environment
use_cases.description: Set up a virtual environment, install project dependencies, and run scripts through uv.

use_cases.title: Standardize Dependency Files
use_cases.description: Create lock files, separate development dependencies, and keep CI installs repeatable.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Package Release
use_cases.description: Review pyproject metadata, build distributions, test installation, and prepare publishing steps.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up a Basic Environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-env skill to help me create a uv-based virtual environment for this Python project. Show the commands and explain what each step changes.

prompt_templates.title: Add Dependencies Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-env skill to add these dependencies with uv. Check whether they belong in runtime or development dependencies before suggesting commands.

prompt_templates.title: Design pyproject.toml
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-env skill to propose a pyproject.toml structure for this project. Include build system, dependencies, optional dev tools, and relevant tool settings.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Release Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-env skill to audit my Python package release workflow. Cover versioning, changelog, build checks, TestPyPI validation, and safer publishing credentials.

output_examples.input: I need a new Python environment for a small API project.
output_examples.output: A concise setup plan with uv environment creation, dependency grouping, and a short verification step.

output_examples.input: My dependency versions drift between local development and CI.
output_examples.output: A lock-file workflow that separates runtime and development inputs, compiles pinned outputs, and syncs CI installs.

output_examples.input: I want to publish my library without leaking tokens.
output_examples.output: A release checklist that favors trusted publishing, TestPyPI validation, build checks, and secret-handling cautions.

best_practices: Review every shell command before running it, especially install and upload commands.
Use lock files and separate development dependencies for repeatable environments.
Prefer trusted publishing or secret managers over tokens in command lines or config examples.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installer scripts directly into a shell without verification.
Do not place real package tokens inside URLs, shell history, or shared configuration files.
Do not run package upload commands until project metadata and target repository are confirmed.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps with uv virtual environments, dependency management, pyproject.toml patterns, and Python package publishing workflows.

faq.question: Does it install uv automatically?
faq.answer: No. It gives installation guidance, but users should choose a verified installation method before running commands.

faq.question: Can it manage private packages?
faq.answer: It shows private index patterns, but real credentials should use secure storage and avoid command-line exposure.

faq.question: Is it safe for production projects?
faq.answer: It can support production workflows, but commands need review because they can install packages and publish artifacts.

faq.question: Does it replace packaging documentation?
faq.answer: No. It provides practical patterns and checklists, while official uv, PyPI, and packaging docs remain authoritative.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill includes a remote shell installer pattern and examples that can expose package credentials if copied directly.
